questions.question: Analyse the importance of land revenue in the British colonial administration.
questions.answer: Land revenue was the main source of income for the British government in India. It helped them finance administration, maintain their army and control the economy.

questions.question: Explain the features of the Permanent Settlement and analyse its impact on rural society.
questions.answer: The Permanent Settlement fixed land revenue permanently and made zamindars landowners responsible for collecting revenue. It changed rural relationships and increased pressure on peasants.

questions.question: Critically examine the role of zamindars in the colonial land revenue system.
questions.answer: Zamindars acted as intermediaries between peasants and the British government. While they collected revenue for the government, many also exploited peasants.

questions.question: Analyse the economic difficulties faced by peasants under the British land revenue system.
questions.answer: Peasants had to pay high taxes, borrow money from moneylenders and often fell into debt, which created economic hardship.

questions.question: Explain how colonial land policies transformed rural society in India.
questions.answer: Colonial policies changed traditional land ownership patterns and created new relationships between landowners, peasants and moneylenders.

questions.question: Analyse the role of moneylenders in the rural economy during colonial rule.
questions.answer: Moneylenders provided loans to peasants but charged high interest rates, trapping many farmers in debt.

questions.question: Explain the importance of indigo cultivation in colonial trade.
questions.answer: Indigo dye was in high demand in European markets, making it an important export crop for colonial trade.

questions.question: Describe the advance system used by indigo planters and its impact on peasants.
questions.answer: Planters gave advance loans to peasants and forced them to grow indigo, which often resulted in low profits and debt.

questions.question: Explain why indigo cultivation was harmful to Indian farmers.
questions.answer: Indigo cultivation reduced the land available for food crops and provided little profit to farmers.

questions.question: Analyse the working conditions of indigo farmers under European planters.
questions.answer: Farmers were forced to grow indigo under harsh conditions and were paid very low prices for their crops.

questions.question: Discuss the causes of the Indigo Revolt.
questions.answer: The revolt was caused by forced indigo cultivation, low profits and exploitation by European planters.

questions.question: Explain how peasants organised resistance during the Indigo Revolt.
questions.answer: Peasants refused to grow indigo, organised protests and challenged the authority of planters.

questions.question: Analyse the significance of the Indigo Revolt in colonial history.
questions.answer: The revolt demonstrated the power of peasant resistance and forced the government to investigate planter practices.

questions.question: Explain the role of the Indigo Commission after the revolt.
questions.answer: The Indigo Commission was formed to investigate the grievances of indigo farmers and examine planter practices.

questions.question: Discuss the impact of indigo cultivation on food production in India.
questions.answer: Large areas of land were used to grow indigo instead of food crops, reducing food production.

questions.question: Explain how colonial economic policies influenced agricultural production.
questions.answer: Colonial policies encouraged the production of export crops rather than food crops.

questions.question: Analyse the relationship between colonial trade and agricultural practices.
questions.answer: Colonial trade created demand for cash crops like indigo, influencing farmers to grow crops for export markets.

questions.question: Explain how peasants resisted colonial agricultural policies.
questions.answer: Peasants protested, refused cultivation and organised revolts such as the Indigo Revolt.

questions.question: Discuss how colonial policies changed rural economic life in India.
questions.answer: New revenue systems increased financial pressure on farmers and altered traditional rural structures.

questions.question: Analyse the role of colonial policies in shaping the agrarian economy.
questions.answer: Colonial policies focused on revenue collection and export crops, which transformed the agrarian economy.

questions.question: Explain why the study of indigo cultivation is important in understanding colonial exploitation.
questions.answer: Indigo cultivation shows how colonial powers exploited farmers to produce crops for international markets.

questions.question: Evaluate the importance of peasant movements in colonial India.
questions.answer: Peasant movements highlighted rural grievances and forced colonial authorities to reconsider some policies.

questions.question: Explain how colonial agricultural policies created long-term economic effects in rural India.
questions.answer: These policies led to debt, poverty and changes in land ownership patterns that affected rural society for many years.

questions.question: Discuss the relationship between colonial administration and rural society.
questions.answer: Colonial administration controlled rural society through revenue systems and economic policies.

questions.question: Analyse the historical importance of the Indigo Revolt in the context of colonial India.
questions.answer: The Indigo Revolt was an important example of organised peasant resistance against colonial exploitation.
